title: Why March Madness is expanding even though no one really wants it to | CNN
text:
Source: CNN

It is always instructive when something bad happens to look back and figure out how in the world we got here.

Today we wake to the mustache painted on the Mona Lisa: The beautifully perfect masterpiece of the NCAA tournament bracket graffitied with an additional eight teams. It would seem, much like Rabbi Noah and Joanne, nobody wants this.

And yet.

So how? How did the very bad, very unnecessary idea of tourney expansion take hold? Quite simply, actually. The bullies threatened to take their ball and go home.

Back when the Big Ten decided to bloat itself from coast to coast, causing an avalanche of conference expansion, the rumblings started about a possible secession. With all the power and money nestled among the football-playing schools – and no NCAA oversight over the football championship – why did those leagues even need the NCAA anymore? Why couldn’t they just create a governance structure of their own and leave everyone else in the dust?

No one really thought they would do it, but even the possible threat that they could started a series of dominoes that equated to the NCAA rolling over to have its belly rubbed. The Power 4 earned a good level of autonomy within the NCAA governance structure, earning the right to require the NCAA to get their approval on major decisions, creating sports-specific silos and even suggesting that they create a new NCAA subdivision unto themselves.

In January 2023, the NCAA also approved a committee recommendation that all sports’ championships ought to include 25% of its teams. The math did not math for a 68-team field for 300-plus basketball teams, and so in the midst of their relatively hostile takeover, the bullies also tossed the leaded balloon about tournament expansion.

The argument was, shockingly, not for the greater good; it was to protect their own entities.

With so many more members in their leagues, teams would beat each other up, bastardizing their conference records and thereby making it harder to earn an at-large bid. Never mind the fact that sports essentially creates a very simple solution to such a problem called competition, wherein good teams – Hi, Florida! – manage just fine with a 16-2 SEC record this season and bad ones – Sorry, Auburn! – with a 7-11 league finish do not.

Nobody thought they’d actually kill the NCAA tournament. In Philadelphia, people still blame Villanova coach Rollie Massimino for killing the Big 5, their beloved city round-robin. Imagine being the villain who kicked Cinderella to the curb.

So instead, they’re just ensuring that Cinderella dances in the hallway, and not the actual ballroom. In its full spin cycle, the NCAA is touting that 21% of college basketball teams will now have a chance to play in the tournament, glossing over the fact that the opportunity is not created equally for everyone.

There will now be eight 16-seeds and six 15s instead of four apiece, which means that a group of low- or mid-major teams will be pushed down their seedline and given harder first-round games.

Worse, traditionally, every 15-seed and two 16s made their way onto the bracket without a so-called play-in game. Now every 16 and half of the 15s will play on Tuesday and Wednesday, with only the winners jetting their way into the first weekend.

In real-world applications that means UMBC might not have even earned the chance to play, let alone upset Virginia, and Saint Peter’s epic Elite Eight run might have been grounded in Dayton.

Instead, perhaps plucky Oklahoma, with all of its financial disadvantages, scheduling struggles and three-star players, could shake up the world.

Your future Cinderella, brought to you by Texas Tech booster Cody Campbell instead of a fairy godmother.

In 2010, Greg Shaheen, then the vice president for men’s basketball, stood before a roomful of reporters at the men’s Final Four and tried to filibuster his way through an argument for a field as large as 96. His opening statement was 2,025 words long (yes, I counted back then). The whole thing went about as you might expect, which is to say terribly. On the backside of that we wound up with 68 teams and the First Four.

We all survived.

We will, of course, survive this, too. The NCAA tournament is too good a product to ruin, despite some true best efforts here.

But the trouble with giving a bully what he wants is he always wants more. Open the henhouse to the fox and he doesn’t just take one egg. There is no evidence that the power conferences will be sated. They have existed like gluttons at an Old Country buffet for years, gorging themselves on anything and everything that fattens their wallets.

They have not, as threatened, taken their ball and gone home but what else will they want to stick around and play?
